channel.title: NYT > Arts > Art & Design
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: $450 Million Worth of Newhouse Trophies Come to Christie’s
item.description: A special May evening sale will feature 16 artworks from the museum-quality collection of the Condé Nast chief S.I. Newhouse Jr.
item.dc:creator: Robin Pogrebin
item.media:credit: Succession Brancusi/All rights reserved (Adagp); Photo by George Etheredge for The New York Times
item.media:description: A Brancusi head in the collection of S.I. Newhouse Jr., expected to sell for $100 million, is decorated with gold leaf.

item.title: This ‘Sustainable’ Island Is Venice’s Newest Art Oasis
item.description: Patrizia Sandretto Re Rebaudengo has turned a former gunpowder store into a showcase for her contemporary art collection.
item.dc:creator: Scott Reyburn
item.media:credit: Matteo de Mayda for The New York Times
item.media:description: Installing Pamela Rosenkranz’s “Old Tree (Pink Seas)” on the island of San Giacomo in Paludo, in Venice.

item.title: First-Timers Try Rooftop Roller Skating at a Children’s Museum
item.description: The Brooklyn Children’s Museum has reimagined a beloved and bygone local rink for its “Empire Skate of Mind” events. Neighborhood kids, many skating for the first time, are lacing up.
item.dc:creator: Vidhya Nagarajan and Sarah Bahr

item.title: How a Museum Doubled Its Attendance in Just One Year
item.description: The Museum of the Moving Image in Queens, long a home for cinephiles, found success, and new visitors, by repositioning itself as a community hub.
item.dc:creator: Melena Ryzik and Gus Aronson

item.title: For Italy’s Art Pioneer, a New Bronze Age
item.description: Giuseppe Penone, veteran of the Arte Povera movement, with his curator Adam Weinberg, former director of the Whitney, are recasting the natural world for a show at Gagosian.
item.dc:creator: Laura Rysman and Emanuele Camerini
item.media:credit: Artists Rights Society (ARS), New York/ADAGP, Paris
item.media:description: The artist Giuseppe Penone at the bronze foundry, Del Chiaro, in Italy, inspecting his sculpture of a tree that was cast in bronze, ahead of the April 22 opening of his solo exhibition at Gagosian in New York.

item.title: Inside LACMA’s Eye-Popping New Home, How Do You Find the Art?
item.description: Our critic calls the David Geffen Galleries “a beacon of glam with brains.” As a space to show art, it has problems. The Latino art is a revelation (if you can navigate the maze).
item.dc:creator: Holland Cotter and Jennelle Fong
item.category: Los Angeles County Museum of Art
item.media:credit: Jennelle Fong for The New York Times

item.title: The Nerve Center of This Art Fair Isn’t Painting. It’s Couture.
item.description: The Independent fair will push boundaries, featuring designs by Rei Kawakubo and Comme des Garçons that blur the line between fashion and sculpture.
item.dc:creator: Tim F. Schneider

item.title: Meet the Turner Prize 2026 Nominees
item.description: This year’s shortlist for the major British art award includes an artist who stages spoken-word performances and another who makes art using oil company merch bought off eBay.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall

item.title: Art Gallery Shows to See in April
item.description: This week in Newly Reviewed, Seph Rodney covers the Metcalf Chateau, Chester Higgins’s evanescent majesty, and paintings that pour onto the walls and floor.
item.dc:creator: Seph Rodney, Andrew Russeth, Jillian Steinhauer and Travis Diehl

item.title: $100 Million Award Made in Suit Over Unlicensed Robert Indiana Art
item.description: A New York jury found that an art publisher who made works derived from Indiana’s images had infringed on the rights of a company that had been the artist’s partner.
item.dc:creator: Graham Bowley
item.media:description: Robert Indiana’s most famous work, “LOVE.” There are iterations of the sculpture in several cities, including this one at John F. Kennedy Plaza in Philadelphia.

item.title: Student Contest Based on Trump’s Garden of Heroes Downplays Darker History
item.description: Guidelines for the art contest, sponsored by the group Freedom 250, include material about abolitionists and civil rights leaders, but little mention of what they fought against.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Schuessler

item.title: The World According to Aldwyth
item.description: In paint, bricolage and collage, she explored the history of art, ideas and the human species.
item.dc:creator: Penelope Green

item.title: Aldwyth, Ascetic Whose Artwork Reordered the World, Dies at 90
item.description: An austere lifestyle allowed her to make epic collages and Joseph Cornell-like contraptions that told stories about art history and her place in — and outside — it.
item.dc:creator: Penelope Green

item.title: The Best Planter Pots for Your Indoor Garden, at a Range of Prices
item.description: When it comes to freshening up a space, these pots pull their weight.
item.dc:creator: Tom Delavan

item.title: In Central Java, an Eco-Resort Aims to Build Sustainability Through Creativity
item.description: When an Indonesian and Australian couple bought Yabbiekayu, their first visitors were foreigners. Now, they aim to be part of a “cultural awakening” in the region.
item.dc:creator: Nina Siegal
item.media:credit: Ulet Ifansasti for The New York Times
item.media:description: A participant during an eco-print workshop at the festival.

item.title: Dutch Panel Designs Plan to Deal With ‘Orphaned’ Nazi-Looted Art
item.description: The panel says a new Jewish foundation, not the government, should take over a massive collection of artworks whose owners have yet to be found.
item.dc:creator: Nina Siegal

item.title: Imagining the Manosphere as a Kinder, Gentler Place
item.description: Two art exhibitions examining hypermasculine online content and its impact argue that sensitivity and vulnerability are also manly virtues.
item.dc:creator: Nina Siegal

item.title: Olafur Eliasson Uses Art and Sound to Raise Climate Awareness in Utah
item.description: The Danish Icelandic artist reflects on his childhood and how it influenced and empowered his art.
item.dc:creator: Farah Nayeri

item.title: The Vienna Climate Biennale Contrasts Chaos with Hope
item.description: In a world marked by ecological, social and political crises, the art festival aims to get people talking, thinking and ready to imagine a better world.
item.dc:creator: Keridwen Cornelius

item.title: A Mural by the Painter of ‘American Gothic’ Gets New Life
item.description: Grant Wood’s 1926 “Corn Room” has been restored and will anchor an exhibition celebrating its centennial at the Sioux City Art Center in Iowa.
item.dc:creator: Alex V. Cipolle
item.media:credit: Jenn Ackerman for The New York Times
item.media:description: From the left, Rita Berg, Tailor Choquette, Alexa Beller, and Kristy Jeffcoat working on Grant Wood’s “Corn Room” mural at the Midwest Art Conservation Center in Minneapolis.

item.title: An Installation in Nature has Climate Lessons for Humans
item.description: With lichen, mechanical artwork and a barrel of snowflakes, “Climate Clock” will wind along a trail through the forests across Oulu, Finland.
item.dc:creator: David Belcher

item.title: The Haas Brothers’ Creative Creations Are on Tour
item.description: A midcareer survey of the work by the twins Simon Haas and Nikolai Haas is touring the country.
item.dc:creator: Ted Loos
item.media:credit: Stella Kalinina for The New York Times

item.title: With $116 Million Gift, National Gallery Will Send Its Art Around Nation
item.description: The large contribution from the billionaire collector Mitchell P. Rales is enabling long-term loans to smaller museums in perpetuity.
item.dc:creator: Robin Pogrebin

item.title: T Magazine’s Latest Salone del Mobile Party Was One to Remember
item.description: This year at T’s annual celebration of Salone del Mobile, Milan’s Villa Necchi Campiglio was transformed with nods to India.
item.dc:creator: Laura May Todd

item.title: Smithsonian American Art Museum Names a New Director
item.description: Lynda Roscoe Hartigan is returning to the museum as its leader after previously serving as its chief curator.
item.dc:creator: Robin Pogrebin

item.title: How an Art Event in the California Desert Became a Community
item.description: As High Desert Test Sites looks toward its 25th anniversary, its founder, and various participants, look back.
item.dc:creator: Lauren Gallow

item.title: An Artist Asks: Without Darkness, Who Are We?
item.description: After gathering ideas from entomologists, neurobiologists and others, Jan Tichy created an exhibition responding to the effects of our disappearing nights.
item.dc:creator: Zoë Lescaze
item.category: Broad, Eli and Edythe, Art Museum (Lansing, Mich)

item.title: An L.A. Artist Devoted to the Process of Paint
item.description: Sandy Rodriguez is a painter but also, on some level, a scientist, an art historian, a botanist, an outdoorswoman, and maybe even an alchemist.
item.dc:creator: Ted Loos
item.media:credit: Stella Kalinina for The New York Times
item.media:description: “I make all my own paint,” said Sandy Rodriguez, 50, who can talk for hours about her process, and, more important to her, the history and meaning of all her choices.

item.title: The Next Frontier in Design? The Humble Birdhouse.
item.description: Recently, as artists and architects have turned their attention outdoors, they have started putting their creative chops to work — for the avians among us.
item.dc:creator: Francesca Perry
item.media:credit: Kirsten Luce for The New York Times

item.title: U.S. Museums Reach Deep into America’s Past
item.description: Museums around the country are celebrating the nation’s heritage in ways that go beyond what might be considered traditional.
item.dc:creator: Alina Tugend
item.category: Crystal Bridges Museum of American Art

item.title: Group Efforts Succeed During Milan Design Week
item.description: Collaborations among designers allow for new creations during the event.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: At Milan Design Week, Function and Form Take on New Meanings
item.description: From a spinning Christmas tree to tables that resemble flowers are among the pieces on display this week.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: Vintage Designs Take on New Lives at Milan Design Week
item.description: Revived pieces, including a 1950s table by Carlo Mollino and a 1960s lamp by Carlo Nason, are on display at the annual festival of furnishings.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: At Milan Design Week, Creative Seating Brings Fresh Ideas to the Table
item.description: A crisp couch, a monolithic chair and a playful pouf highlight the ways designers are reimagining how we recline.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: A Fountain of Creativity from Arid Uzbekistan
item.description: A design exhibition inspired by the Central Asian republic’s cultural traditions makes its debut in the heart of Milan.
item.dc:creator: Ifeoluwa Adedeji

item.title: How Two Men with Hard Heads Broke Through Murano’s Glass Ceiling
item.description: The founders of the glass brand 6:AM bring a punk attitude to the production of dazzling pieces.
item.dc:creator: Laura May Todd
item.media:credit: Matteo de Mayda for The New York Times
item.media:description: Edoardo Pandolfo, left, and Francesco Palù, founders of the glass brand 6:AM. The two create custom Murano glass installations, while producing their own furniture, lighting and objects.

item.title: With Trump Novices, Can the U.S. Win the ‘Art Olympics’?
item.description: After the State Department overhauled the process for choosing an artist for the Venice Biennale, it gave control to a woman who previously owned a pet food store.
item.dc:creator: Zachary Small

item.title: A Time of Growth for Museums for Children
item.description: Across the nation, news museums are opening, and existing ones are expanding.
item.dc:creator: Shivani Vora
item.category: Kansas Children's Discovery Center (Topeka, Kan, Museum)

item.title: A New Museum in California Aims to Draw Children to Science
item.description: The $7 million KidSTREAM museum was the brainchild of a former teacher looking for a place to entertain her young daughters.
item.dc:creator: Shivani Vora
item.media:credit: Adam Amengual for The New York Times
item.media:description: A whales at play sculpture at the KidSTREAM museum in Camarillo, Calif.
